evals.prompt: I have a Python file at evals/files/test-file-with-secrets.py that might contain hardcoded secrets. Can you scan it with gitleaks to check? Use the gitleaks dir mode and report what you find.
evals.expected_output: Gitleaks should detect multiple hardcoded secrets in the file (AWS secret key, GitHub token, API keys, Slack token, private key) and report them with file locations and rule IDs.
expectations: Detected the AWS secret key pattern (aws-access-token or similar)
Detected the GitHub personal access token (github-pat or similar)
Detected the generic API key (generic-api-key or similar)
Reported results in JSON or readable format
Used the gitleaks dir command via podman container

evals.prompt: Run a gitleaks secret scan on the current directory (skills/gitleaks-scan/) to verify no secrets are present. Skip any findings in evals/ if they show up. Use the dir mode and give me a JSON report.
evals.expected_output: Gitleaks should find either no secrets or only the known test fixtures in evals/files/ and report clean results.
expectations: Ran gitleaks on the specified directory
Properly handled the exclusions or reported findings accurately

evals.prompt: I need a custom .gitleaks.toml configuration file that extends the default rules and adds a custom rule for detecting my company's internal API tokens. The tokens start with 'MYCO-' followed by 20 hex characters. Also should ignore the evals/ directory globally. Write the .gitleaks.toml file and then run gitleaks with it to validate the config works.
evals.expected_output: A valid .gitleaks.toml file with extend config, a custom rule for MYCO- tokens, and a global allowlist for evals/. Gitleaks should load and validate the config successfully.
expectations: Created a valid .gitleaks.toml with [extend] and useDefault = true
Added a custom [[rules]] section for MYCO- prefixed tokens
Added a global [[allowlists]] section for evals/ directory
Ran gitleaks with the config to validate it works
Explained how to use the custom config in future scans

evals.prompt: Show me how to set up an incremental gitleaks scanning workflow using baselines. First create a baseline report from scanning evals/files/test-file-with-secrets.py, then simulate a new scan that uses the baseline to only show new findings.
evals.expected_output: Demonstration of creating a baseline JSON report and then running gitleaks with --baseline-path to filter previously-known findings.
expectations: Created a baseline by running gitleaks on the test file
Demonstrated knowledge of how baselines filter findings
Used the gitleaks container via podman
